title: Working with Dictionaries
instructions: Complete the functions to practice dictionary operations: counting, transforming, and querying data.
hints: Use dict.get(key, default) to handle missing keys
Dictionary comprehensions can create new dictionaries
The max() function can take a key parameter for custom comparison

testCases.description: count_words should count occurrences of each word (case insensitive)
testCases.validation: assert count_words('the cat and the dog') == {'the': 2, 'cat': 1, 'and': 1, 'dog': 1}, 'count_words should count word frequencies'

testCases.description: count_words should treat uppercase and lowercase as the same word
testCases.validation: assert count_words('Hello hello HELLO') == {'hello': 3}, 'count_words should be case insensitive'

testCases.description: invert_dict should swap keys and values
testCases.validation: assert invert_dict({'x': 1, 'y': 2}) == {1: 'x', 2: 'y'}, 'invert_dict should swap keys and values'

testCases.description: merge_dicts should combine dicts with dict2 overriding shared keys
testCases.validation: assert merge_dicts({'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'b': 9, 'c': 3}) == {'a': 1, 'b': 9, 'c': 3}, 'merge_dicts should override with dict2 values'

testCases.description: get_top_score should return the name with the highest score
testCases.validation: assert get_top_score({'Alice': 85, 'Bob': 92, 'Carol': 78}) == 'Bob', 'get_top_score should return Bob with score 92'

testCases.description: get_top_score should return None for an empty dictionary
testCases.validation: assert get_top_score({}) is None, 'get_top_score({}) should return None'

description: Practice core dictionary operations in Python. Build a word frequency counter using get() with defaults, invert dictionaries with comprehensions, merge dictionaries with copy() and update(), and find maximum values using max() with a key function. These are common real-world patterns for counting, transforming, and querying dictionary data.
